timeline.event: Born in Kaysersberg, Alsace, as the eldest son of a Lutheran pastor.
Studied at the village school.
Began studying at the Realschule (secondary school).
Earned a PhD in philosophy from the University of Strasbourg with a study on Kant.
Became a pastor in Strasbourg.
Earned a PhD in theology with a study on the life of Jesus and early Christianity.
Became a lecturer at the theological faculty of his alma mater.
Entered medical school at the age of 30.
Cooperated in founding the Paris Bach Society and also served as an organist.
Began medical volunteer work with his wife, Helene Bresslau, in Lambaréné, French Equatorial Africa.
Published his theological book 'The Quest of the Historical Jesus'.
His mother died after being hit by military horses of French soldiers.
Imprisoned in a French prisoner-of-war camp.
Changed his nationality from German to French.
Returned to Lambaréné, Africa after fundraising in Europe.
Published his autobiography 'Out of My Life and Thought'.
Received the Nobel Peace Prize.
Received the British honorary Order of Merit (OM).
Engaged in an anti-nuclear movement opposing nuclear weapons through radio broadcasts and newspaper articles.
Died at the age of 90 in Lambaréné.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Peace Prize
keyAchievements.description: He received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1952 for his contributions to medical volunteer work in Africa and the development of human brotherhood, based on his philosophy of 'Reverence for Life'.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Albert Schweitzer Hospital
keyAchievements.description: He established the Albert Schweitzer Hospital in Lambaréné, Gabon, in the western region of Central Africa.

keyAchievements.title: Activities as a Musician and Organist
keyAchievements.description: He studied the German composer Johann Sebastian Bach, influenced the organ reform movement in the early 20th century, and served as an organist for the Paris Bach Society.

keyAchievements.title: Earned PhDs in Philosophy and Theology
keyAchievements.description: He earned a PhD in philosophy for his study on Kant and a PhD in theology for his study on the life of Jesus and early Christianity from the University of Strasbourg.

keyAchievements.title: Dedication to Medical Volunteer Work in Africa
keyAchievements.description: From 1913, he dedicated his life to medical work for Black people in Lambaréné, French Equatorial Africa, alongside his wife.

keyAchievements.title: Advocated the Philosophy of 'Reverence for Life'
keyAchievements.description: He advocated the philosophy of 'Reverence for Life', stating that all life is sacred and no life should be sacrificed, in his book 'Out of My Life and Thought'.

keyAchievements.title: Anti-nuclear Peace Movement
keyAchievements.description: In 1957, he engaged in an anti-nuclear movement opposing nuclear weapons through radio broadcasts and newspaper articles, greatly influencing the peace movement.

faq.question: Who was Albert Schweitzer?
faq.answer: Albert Schweitzer was a German-born French physician, musician, philosopher, theologian, and Lutheran pastor who dedicated himself to medical volunteer work in Africa based on his philosophy of 'Reverence for Life' and received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1952.

faq.question: What is Schweitzer's philosophy of 'Reverence for Life'?
faq.answer: 'Reverence for Life' is a Christian idea that all life is sacred and no life should be sacrificed, which he advocated and practiced in his book 'Out of My Life and Thought'.

faq.question: Why did Schweitzer start medical volunteer work in Africa?
faq.answer: Schweitzer believed it was right to share happiness with everyone, and maturing with the realization that 'I cannot live happily alone,' he entered medical school at age 30 and subsequently began medical volunteer work in Africa.

faq.question: What kind of musical activities did Schweitzer do?
faq.answer: Schweitzer was an organist and Bach researcher; he helped establish the Paris Bach Society, served as an organist, and held concert tours in Europe to raise funds for his volunteer work in Africa.

faq.question: When did Schweitzer receive the Nobel Peace Prize?
faq.answer: Schweitzer received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1952 for his philosophy of 'Reverence for Life' and his contributions to medical volunteer work in Africa and the advancement of human brotherhood.

faq.question: How did Schweitzer's nationality change?
faq.answer: The Alsace region where Schweitzer was born was a site of intense territorial disputes between Germany and France, and he changed his nationality from German to French in 1919.

timeline.event: Converted to Christianity while staying in England.
Was one of the 10 female delegates at the Congress session.
Established the Mukti Mission, a Christian charity, in the village of Kedgaon, 40 miles east of Pune.

keyAchievements.title: Acquiring Titles
keyAchievements.description: After passing examinations by the faculty of Calcutta University, she became the first woman to receive the titles of Pandita and Sarasvati as a Sanskrit scholar.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Sharada Sadan
keyAchievements.description: She started Sharada Sadan, a home for widows, using raised funds.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Mukti Mission
keyAchievements.description: She established the Mukti Mission, a Christian charity, in the village of Kedgaon, 40 miles east of Pune.

faq.question: What is Pandita Ramabai's real name?
faq.answer: Her real name is Pandita Ramabai Sarasvati.

faq.question: What titles did Pandita Ramabai receive?
faq.answer: After passing examinations by the faculty of Calcutta University, she received the titles of Pandita and Sarasvati as a Sanskrit scholar.

faq.question: What are the major institutions established by Pandita Ramabai?
faq.answer: She established Sharada Sadan for widows and the Mukti Mission, a Christian charity.

timeline.event: Born in Bristol, Gloucestershire, England.
Entire family immigrated to New York, USA.
Family settled in Cincinnati, Ohio.
Admitted to Geneva Medical College in New York state.
Graduated top of her class from Geneva Medical College.
Dedicated herself to nursing and treating wounded soldiers upon the outbreak of the American Civil War.
Founded a medical college in New York state.
Founded a medical college in London.
Died.

keyAchievements.title: Graduated Top of Her Class from Geneva Medical College
keyAchievements.description: In 1849, she graduated at the top of her class from Geneva Medical College in New York, becoming a doctor.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Medical Colleges
keyAchievements.description: She established a medical college in New York in 1865 and in London in 1874.

keyAchievements.title: Medical Activities During the Civil War
keyAchievements.description: When the American Civil War broke out in 1861, she dedicated herself to nursing and treating wounded soldiers.

faq.question: Who was Elizabeth Blackwell?
faq.answer: Elizabeth Blackwell was an English physician born in 1821 and died in 1910.

faq.question: When did Elizabeth Blackwell decide to become a doctor?
faq.answer: She decided to become a doctor in Cincinnati, Ohio, under the influence of Harriet Beecher Stowe.

faq.question: What kind of education did Elizabeth Blackwell receive?
faq.answer: She entered Geneva Medical College in New York state in 1847 and graduated at the top of her class in 1849.

faq.question: What medical institutions did Elizabeth Blackwell establish?
faq.answer: She established a medical college in New York in 1865 and in London in 1874.

timeline.event: Born in Scotland as the son of a working-class family.
Departed for Africa as a medical missionary for the London Missionary Society.
Arrived in Botswana, South Africa, and began missionary work.
Discovered Lake Ngami during his first expedition.
Discovered the Zambezi River during his first expedition.
Discovered Victoria Falls during his first expedition and successfully crossed Africa.
Became a British consul and led an expedition to Central Africa, exploring the Zambezi River basin and liberating slaves.
Commissioned by the Royal Geographical Society, he left for Africa again and discovered Lake Mweru and Lake Bangweulu.
Contracted a fever in Ujiji on the shores of Lake Tanganyika but was rescued by Henry Morton Stanley's search expedition.
Died of extreme exhaustion and illness on the shores of Lake Bangweulu.

keyAchievements.title: Exploration and Discoveries in Africa
keyAchievements.description: From 1849 to 1856, he crossed the Kalahari Desert, discovered Lake Ngami, the Zambezi River, and Victoria Falls, successfully crossing the African continent.

keyAchievements.title: Anti-Slavery Activities
keyAchievements.description: In 1858, he intervened in the slave trade by the Portuguese in the Zambezi River basin, liberating hundreds of slaves.

keyAchievements.title: Geographical Exploration of Central Africa
keyAchievements.description: Commissioned by the Royal Geographical Society, he continued his exploration of Central Africa, discovering lakes such as Mweru and Bangweulu.

keyAchievements.title: Publication of Exploration Records
keyAchievements.description: After returning from his African explorations, he published 'Missionary Travels', detailing his experiences.

faq.question: When and where was David Livingstone born?
faq.answer: David Livingstone was born in Scotland on March 19, 1813.

faq.question: What was David Livingstone's main profession?
faq.answer: He was a Scottish Congregationalist missionary and explorer.

faq.question: What major discoveries did David Livingstone make in Africa?
faq.answer: He discovered Lake Ngami, the Zambezi River, and Victoria Falls, and successfully crossed the Kalahari Desert and the African continent.

faq.question: How did David Livingstone die, and what happened to his body?
faq.answer: He died on May 1, 1873, on the shores of Lake Bangweulu. His heart was buried in Zambia, and his embalmed body was given a state funeral at Westminster Abbey in London.

faq.question: What social activities did David Livingstone engage in while in Africa?
faq.answer: He intervened in the slave trade by the Portuguese in the Zambezi River basin, freeing hundreds of slaves.

timeline.event: Birth of Alfred Bernhard Nobel.
Nobel's father moved the family to St. Petersburg, Russia.
Went to Paris, France to continue his studies.
Went to the United States to study for a year at the age of 18.
Filed his first patent for a gas meter at the British Patent Office.
Nobel's father left the factory to his second son, Ludvig Nobel.
Received his first Swedish patent for 'a method of preparing gunpowder'.
Invented the detonator.
An explosion at the Heleneborg factory in Stockholm killed five people, including his younger brother Emil.
Designed the blasting cap, an improvement on the detonator.
Invented dynamite and received patents in the US and UK.
Demonstrated dynamite for the first time at a quarry in Redhill, Surrey, England.
Invented gelignite.
Received a patent for gelignite.
Elected a member of the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences.
Patented ballistite.
Alfred Nobel's brother Ludvig died, leading some newspapers to mistakenly publish Alfred Nobel's obituary.
Received an honorary doctorate from Uppsala University.
Finally signed his will at the Swedish-Norwegian Club in Paris, establishing the Nobel Prize.
Died at his villa in Sanremo, Italy.
The Nobel Prize system was established.
The Sveriges Riksbank donated to the Nobel Foundation to establish the 'Sveriges Riksbank Prize in Economic Sciences in Memory of Alfred Nobel'.
Alfred Nobel's great-grandnephew Peter Nobel requested that the Economics Prize be distinguished from the original Nobel Prizes.
The Nobel Foundation holds investment assets of approximately 6 billion Swedish kronor.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Dynamite
keyAchievements.description: He invented dynamite, a solid explosive, and received a patent for it in 1867.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Gelignite
keyAchievements.description: In 1875, he invented gelignite, which was more stable and powerful than dynamite, and patented it in 1876.

keyAchievements.title: Invention of Ballistite
keyAchievements.description: In 1887, he patented ballistite, a type of smokeless powder.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of the Nobel Prize
keyAchievements.description: He established the Nobel Prize with his fortune to annually award those who 'conferred the greatest benefit to humankind'.

keyAchievements.title: Held 355 Patents
keyAchievements.description: During his lifetime, he made numerous important contributions to science, registering a total of 355 patents internationally.

keyAchievements.title: Growth of Bofors
keyAchievements.description: He transformed Bofors, originally an iron and steel company, into a major manufacturer of cannons and armaments.

keyAchievements.title: Design of Detonators and Blasting Caps
keyAchievements.description: He invented the detonator in 1863 and designed the blasting cap, an improvement on the detonator, in 1865.

faq.question: What inventions is Alfred Nobel famous for?
faq.answer: He invented dynamite, a solid explosive, as well as gelignite and ballistite.

faq.question: How did the Nobel Prize begin?
faq.answer: Upon his death, Nobel donated most of his wealth to establish a fund to run the Nobel Prize, which annually awards those who have 'conferred the greatest benefit to humankind'.

faq.question: How many patents did Nobel hold during his lifetime?
faq.answer: Nobel registered a total of 355 patents internationally during his life.

faq.question: When did Alfred Nobel die?
faq.answer: Nobel died on December 10, 1896, at his villa in Sanremo, Italy.

faq.question: What things are named after Alfred Nobel?
faq.answer: The synthetic element nobelium was named after him, and there is a Nobel Prize Museum in Stockholm dedicated to his life and the Nobel Prize.

timeline.event: Born in Paris as Charles Pierre de Frédy, Baron de Coubertin.
Announced a plan to revive the Olympic Games and declared it at the congress of the Union des Sociétés Françaises de Sports Athlétiques. Officiated the French Rugby Union championship.
Organized the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and became its Secretary General.
Held the first modern Olympic Games in Athens, Greece, and assumed the position of IOC President.
The Olympics were held in Paris but were overshadowed by the World's Fair.
The Olympics were held in St. Louis but were overshadowed by the World's Fair.
Created the modern pentathlon for the Summer Olympics.
Stepped down as IOC President after the Summer Olympics held in Lausanne.
Was a nominee for the Nobel Peace Prize but did not win.
Died of a stroke in Geneva on September 2.

keyAchievements.title: Founder of the Modern Olympics
keyAchievements.description: He founded the modern Olympic Games with the idea of contributing to world peace by reviving the ancient Olympic Games.

keyAchievements.title: Organization and Presidency of the International Olympic Committee (IOC)
keyAchievements.description: In 1894, he organized the IOC and became its Secretary General, later serving as its President and laying the foundation for the modern Olympics.

keyAchievements.title: Development of the Olympic Charter and Protocol
keyAchievements.description: He developed the Olympic Charter and protocols, including the procedures for the opening and closing ceremonies.

keyAchievements.title: Design of the Olympic Rings Symbol
keyAchievements.description: He designed the Olympic rings, the symbol of the Olympics.

keyAchievements.title: Creation of the Modern Pentathlon
keyAchievements.description: He created the modern pentathlon for the 1912 Summer Olympics.

keyAchievements.title: Organization of the Universal Pedagogical Union
keyAchievements.description: He organized the Universal Pedagogical Union and was appointed as its honorary president for life.

faq.question: Who was Pierre de Coubertin?
faq.answer: He was a French educator and the founder of the modern Olympic Games.

faq.question: What was the main motive behind founding the modern Olympics?
faq.answer: While pursuing educational reform, he realized the importance of sports, and the excavation of the Olympia ruins by German archaeologists inspired him to revive the ancient Olympics.

faq.question: What was his role in the International Olympic Committee (IOC)?
faq.answer: He organized the IOC in 1894, became its first Secretary General, and subsequently served as its President, leading the Olympic movement.

faq.question: What is the significance of the Pierre de Coubertin medal?
faq.answer: It is an award given to athletes who demonstrate true sportsmanship at the Olympics, considered one of the highest honors an Olympic athlete can receive.

faq.question: What did he aim to achieve through the Olympics?
faq.answer: He sought to contribute to world peace by bringing together the youth of different countries through the Olympic Games.

timeline.event: Born in Store Frøen.
Made his first voyage to the waters off Greenland.
Received a doctorate from the University of Christiania for his thesis 'The Structure and Combination of the Histological Elements of the Central Nervous System'.
Crossed the Greenland ice cap on skis from east to west along with Otto Sverdrup and others.
Attempted to cross the Arctic Ocean aboard the Fram, an exploration ship he designed and built.
Reached a latitude of 86° 14' N with Hjalmar Johansen, recording the northernmost point reached by humans at the time.
After wintering in Franz Josef Land, met a British expedition led by Frederick George Jackson and returned.
Appointed and served as the Norwegian Ambassador to the United Kingdom.
Received the Nobel Peace Prize for his activities as the High Commissioner of the League of Nations.
Co-founded the anti-socialist political organization Fedrelandslaget (Fatherland League).
Ended his life in Lysaker, near Oslo.

keyAchievements.title: Crossing the Greenland Ice Cap
keyAchievements.description: In 1888, he crossed the Greenland ice cap on skis from east to west with Otto Sverdrup and others.

keyAchievements.title: Arctic Exploration and Reaching the Farthest North
keyAchievements.description: He attempted to cross the Arctic Ocean on the Fram, which he designed and built, and in 1895 reached 86° 14' N, the northernmost latitude reached by man at the time.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to the Neuron Doctrine
keyAchievements.description: He became one of the founders of the neuron doctrine by arguing that the neural network consists of individual communicating cells.

keyAchievements.title: Oceanographic Research
keyAchievements.description: Based on his experiences on the Fram, he studied the nature and causes of ocean currents and served as a professor of oceanography at the University of Oslo.

keyAchievements.title: Contributions to Norwegian Independence and Diplomacy
keyAchievements.description: He played a major role in persuading Prince Carl of Denmark to accept the Norwegian throne, and worked as the Norwegian Ambassador to the UK to secure British support for Norway's territorial integrity.

keyAchievements.title: Refugee Relief Work and the Nansen Passport
keyAchievements.description: As the League of Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, he engaged in humanitarian work such as repatriating prisoners of war, undertaking refugee relief, and issuing the Nansen passport for refugees.

keyAchievements.title: Nobel Peace Prize
keyAchievements.description: He was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize in 1922 for his humanitarian work with the League of Nations.

faq.question: Who was Fridtjof Nansen?
faq.answer: Fridtjof Nansen was a Norwegian explorer, scientist, and diplomat who received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1922 for his activities as the League of Nations High Commissioner.

faq.question: What are Fridtjof Nansen's main exploration achievements?
faq.answer: He crossed the Greenland ice cap on skis in 1888 and attempted to cross the Arctic Ocean on the Fram in 1893, reaching the then-northernmost point of 86° 14' N in 1895.

faq.question: What were Fridtjof Nansen's academic contributions?
faq.answer: He was one of the founders of the neuron doctrine, stating that the neural network is composed of individual cells. He also studied the properties and origins of ocean currents and served as a professor of zoology and oceanography at the University of Oslo.

faq.question: Why did Fridtjof Nansen receive the Nobel Peace Prize?
faq.answer: He received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1922 for contributing to humanitarian work as the League of Nations High Commissioner for Refugees, including repatriating prisoners of war, undertaking refugee relief, and issuing Nansen passports.

faq.question: What is the Nansen passport?
faq.answer: The Nansen passport was an internationally recognized travel document issued to refugees by Fridtjof Nansen during his time as the League of Nations High Commissioner for Refugees.

timeline.event: Birth of George Eastman (July 12)
Applied for US Patent 226,503 'Method and Apparatus for Coating Plates' (September)
US Patent 226,503 'Method and Apparatus for Coating Plates' issued (April)
David H. Houston's US Patent 248,179 'Photographic Apparatus' (roll film holder) applied for (June 21)
David H. Houston's US Patent 248,179 'Photographic Apparatus' (roll film holder) issued (October 11)
Applied for US Patent 306,594 'Photographic Film' (March 7)
Applied for US Patent 306,470 'Photographic Film' (May 10)
Applied for US Patent 317,049 (jointly with William H. Walker) 'Roll Holder for Photographic Films' (August)
US Patent 306,470 'Photographic Film' issued (October 14)
US Patent 306,594 'Photographic Film' issued (October 14)
US Patent 317,049 (jointly with William H. Walker) 'Roll Holder for Photographic Films' issued (May)
Applied for US Patent 388,850 'Camera' (March)
US Patent 388,850 'Camera' issued (September)
Roll film became the basis for the invention of motion picture film
Death of George Eastman (March 14)

keyAchievements.title: Founding of Eastman Kodak
keyAchievements.description: He founded the company known as Eastman Kodak.

keyAchievements.title: Launch of Roll Film and Popularization of Photography
keyAchievements.description: He launched roll film, contributing to bringing photography into the mainstream.

keyAchievements.title: Laying the Foundation for Motion Picture Film
keyAchievements.description: Roll film became the basis for the invention of motion picture film in 1888.

keyAchievements.title: George Eastman Museum Designated as a National Historic Landmark
keyAchievements.description: The George Eastman Museum was designated as a National Historic Landmark in the United States.

keyAchievements.title: Two Stars on the Hollywood Walk of Fame
keyAchievements.description: He is the only person to be represented by two stars on the Hollywood Walk of Fame.

faq.question: Which company is George Eastman associated with?
faq.answer: George Eastman founded the company called Eastman Kodak.

faq.question: What are George Eastman's major achievements?
faq.answer: He contributed to popularizing photography by launching roll film, which also served as the basis for the invention of motion picture film in 1888.

faq.question: What is the significance of the George Eastman Museum?
faq.answer: The George Eastman Museum is designated as a National Historic Landmark in the United States.

timeline.event: Birth of Elisha Graves Otis (August 3)
Moved to New York at the age of 19, leaving his home.
Invented an elevator equipped with a safety device.
Founded Otis Elevator in New York.
Publicly demonstrated the elevator with a safety device at the New York World's Fair.
Otis Elevator installed the first passenger elevator in New York.
Received a patent related to elevator safety but died shortly after (April 8).

keyAchievements.title: Invention of the Modern Elevator
keyAchievements.description: He invented the elevator with a safety device, laying the foundation for modern elevators.

keyAchievements.title: Establishment of Otis Elevator
keyAchievements.description: He founded the Otis Elevator Company in New York in 1853.

keyAchievements.title: Public Demonstration of the Safety Elevator
keyAchievements.description: At the 1854 New York World's Fair, he publicly demonstrated the operation of his safety device by cutting the hoisting rope of an ascending elevator.

keyAchievements.title: Installation of the First Passenger Elevator
keyAchievements.description: Through Otis Elevator, the first passenger elevator was installed in New York in 1857.

faq.question: What is Elisha Otis famous for?
faq.answer: He is the inventor of the modern elevator and the founder of the Otis Elevator Company.

faq.question: When did Elisha Otis invent the safety elevator?
faq.answer: He invented the elevator equipped with a safety device in 1851.

faq.question: When was the Otis Elevator Company founded?
faq.answer: The Otis Elevator Company was founded in New York in 1853.

faq.question: How did Elisha Otis demonstrate the safety elevator?
faq.answer: At the 1854 New York World's Fair, he demonstrated the operation of the safety device by having the hoisting rope cut while he stood on an ascending elevator.

timeline.event: Dispatched to China with Yun Sa-ung, Choi Cheon-gu, and others to learn the designs of astronomical instruments.

Freed from his status as a government slave in recognition of his contributions to manufacturing astronomical instruments, and appointed as an official of the 5th junior rank at the Royal Bureau of Attire.
Promoted to the 5th senior rank and ordered to manufacture the Gaengjeomjigi, the first water clock.
Granted the military rank of Hogun (4th senior rank).
Completed the Jagyeokru, an automatic water clock powered by water.
Participated in the casting of the Gabinja, a movable metal type, under the general direction of Yi Cheon.
Completed the Ongnu, an astronomical clock, and installed it in the Heumgyeonggak pavilion.

keyAchievements.title: Jagyeokru
keyAchievements.description: He completed the Jagyeokru, a hydro-powered automatic water clock that strikes the time.

keyAchievements.title: Ongnu
keyAchievements.description: He manufactured the Ongnu, an astronomical clock that displays the movement of the sun and moon as models, allowing observation of time, seasons, and celestial movements.

keyAchievements.title: Astronomical Instruments
keyAchievements.description: He produced various astronomical observation instruments such as the Daeganui, Soganui, Hyeonju-ilgu, Cheonpyeong-ilgu, Jeongnam-ilgu, Angbu-ilgu, Ilseongjeongsiui, and Gyupyo.

keyAchievements.title: Gabinja
keyAchievements.description: He contributed to the advancement of printing technology by participating in the casting of the Gabinja, a movable metal type made of copper.

faq.question: What is Jang Yeong-sil's birth year?
faq.answer: Jang Yeong-sil's birth year is not clearly recorded, but it is estimated that he was born between 1389 and 1390, judging from records indicating he was about 34 years old when he was freed from slavery in 1423.

faq.question: What was Jang Yeong-sil's social status?
faq.answer: Jang Yeong-sil was originally a government slave of Dongnae-hyeon, but his outstanding technical skills were recognized, and he was promoted by King Taejong and King Sejong.

faq.question: Why did Jang Yeong-sil step down from his official position?
faq.answer: Due to an incident where King Sejong's palanquin broke, he was accused of disrespect to the King, faced dismissal from office, and subsequently disappeared from history.

faq.question: What were Jang Yeong-sil's family relations?
faq.answer: His father was an immigrant from the Yuan dynasty, originally from Suzhou/Hangzhou, and his mother was a gisaeng from Dongnae-hyeon, Joseon. He had one younger sister, and his younger female cousin is said to have married the astronomer Kim Dam.
